_note: v5: maximally descriptive, game-faithful prompting + richer vary values. v4 NAMED the game but leaned on the base checkpoint to know it, so game-anchored subjects still drifted generic. v5 stacks official-key-art language, render/quality descriptors, and descriptive vary options so the batch reads as THAT game and pops on a YouTube grid. CEILING: a base SDXL checkpoint has no memory of a specific title's creatures — for true screenshot-accuracy add a game LoRA (LoRA field) or feed a real source image. The checkpoint (juggernautXL) is already a top thumbnail model; fidelity to a specific game is a LoRA/reference job, not a base-model job.
template: official promotional YouTube thumbnail key art for the video game {game}: {subject}, unmistakably from {game}, {emotion} facial expression, {composition}, authentic {game} creatures, environment and interface in the background, faithful to {game}'s signature art style and color identity, {palette} palette, {lighting}, ultra-detailed cinematic game render, crisp high contrast, punchy saturated colors, sharp focus, professional marketing key art, clean uncluttered zone left for a large title
negative: text, letters, words, typography, caption, watermark, signature, logo, blurry, soft, out of focus, low contrast, washed out, dull, muddy colors, extra limbs, extra fingers, deformed hands, cropped body, generic stock fantasy, unrelated game, amateur, flat lifeless lighting

lighting: dramatic rim light separating the subject from the background
hard on-camera flash with deep shadows
composition: subject filling the left third with the face large and close
extreme close-up hero shot with negative space on the right

rubric.game_reference: 1-5 — is THIS game unmistakable (its real creatures, environment, UI, art style)?
rubric.hook: 1-5 — would this stop a scroll on YouTube?
